intro: How our thinking has changed over time — the evolution from an initial concept toward what will become the MVP. The learning log below is added to constantly as each pilot teaches us more; the open questions up top are the ones we're still pressure-testing.

questions.question: Would an agent actually send what Upline drafts?
questions.note: Agency rapport is warming and back-channel signals say the team is 'way more excited than they're letting on.' The real test is still the post-shop recommendation email.

questions.question: Do customers respond and opt in?
questions.note: Early yes: questionnaires completed fast (under 8 minutes), ~25% response on a batch, and a life-insurance opt-in already booked a meeting. Directionally meaningful, not crickets.

questions.question: Is automated shopping the wedge?
questions.note: No. Shopping is a ~$6 task; retention and cross-sell are the ~$800 tasks — and only they serve the mantra. Lead with those; let VAs shop for now.

questions.question: Can this become a real, scalable product?
questions.note: Path changed rather than closed: VAs shop in the near term while we build the data repository; full shopping automation is a 2-year horizon, not a blocker.

questions.question: How do we price when agencies control the copy and the cohort?
questions.note: Both outcome-only and book-based pricing have failure modes. Pricing must assume agencies won't use the product exactly as we recommend.

questions.question: Can we operate without ongoing AMS integration?
questions.note: Working hypothesis: a one-time AMS dump on onboarding, then carrier portals + email/questionnaire extraction keep our own system of record current. Research in progress.

entries.learning: The durable wedge is clarity and proactive communication at renewal — not the lowest rate. Renewals are the highest-leverage moment for an agent to prove value, but a ~1,500-policy book throws off ~30 renewals a week, far too many to work account-by-account by hand today.

entries.title: Built the Upline concept demo
entries.learning: We created the Upline concept demo — a clickable experience showing what a renewal handled by Upline could feel like — and put it in front of the Members 1st team.

The concept resonated org-wide. The excitement wasn't limited to agency owners and producers — the individual support and service teams were visibly bought in too. Strong enough signal to commit to a live pilot.

Real agency data is messier than expected. The AMS and the documents disagree, duplicate, and go stale, and a missing or bad email is a hard stop for outreach. The renewal premium also has to be posted in the AMS before we can act — and many clients 45–60 days out aren't posted yet — so the workable outreach window is narrower than we assumed.
detail: Selection filters (home + auto, verified email, premium posted, under 80) shrink the list so much they nearly pick the cohort for us.

entries.learning: We stripped the UI and built the logic in Cursor first. Product-market fit here is 'would I actually send that email / is that a good proposal?' — not the interface. We stay un-beholden to any UI until the underlying logic is proven.

entries.title: Members 1st pilot kicked off
entries.learning: Our first live pilot with an independent agency began — weekly Mon/Wed/Fri working sessions, and the first real outreach going out under the agency's own name.

After a lot of back-and-forth with the Members 1st team, we landed on a single boilerplate outreach email in the agency's exact voice — not the customized, circumstance-based copy we started with. Its only job is to give a little context that sets up the shopping conversation.
detail: Show the new premium only — never the old price, the % increase, or a cause diagnosis (clients read the jump as a reason to leave: 'let me go down to State Farm').
Open with the broker identity — a valued member of an agency that shops ~7 carriers on the client's behalf.
One email for everyone, regardless of increase / flat / decrease — the V1 circumstance logic was removed (~85% rewritten).

entries.learning: Concentrate shopping where the retention risk is. Shop the biggest increases first (15%+, ranked largest-first) — the big jumps bleed out the fastest. Email everyone on renewal as a relationship touch, but put the shopping effort behind the largest increases; phone follow-up stays in-house to keep it personal.
With outreach reduced to formatting and sending, the product's value moves downstream. The intelligence has to live in the questionnaire → shop → recommendation, not in the outreach email.

Pricing has to assume agencies won't use the product exactly as we recommend — they'll change the copy and narrow the cohort. Outcome-only pricing risks results we can't attribute; book-based pricing invites agencies to shrink outreach. Still an open question.

First real end-to-end signal: a client went all the way through shopping, beyond just draft review. Engagement is landing too — questionnaires complete in under 8 minutes, and even the plain boilerplate email draws responses (arguably a cleaner signal, since the copy isn't doing the persuading).

The pivot: de-prioritize automated shopping in favor of VAs guided step-by-step ('Uber drivers, not taxi drivers'). Automated quoting by RPA is harder than hoped — the real gap is an incomplete question set, not just brittle automation — so we'll be our own VAs for at least the first year. Moving the shopping cost onto us is fine; the value is in retention and cross-sell.
detail: RPA friction seen live: dropdowns not populating, plus carrier edge cases like roof and furnace age.

entries.learning: The mantra settles the strategy. Automated shopping just saves the agent time — a ~$6 task — while retention and cross-sell are the ~$800 tasks that actually prove the agent is in the customer's corner. Lead with those, and build the data repository as a byproduct of using Upline; after ~24 months of accumulated data it can surface as a superior alternative to the AMS.

Cross-sell is a real revenue line, not a someday idea. A life-insurance batch drew ~25% responses; 4 of 5 questionnaire respondents asked about life and one booked a meeting — and agencies pay ~$200 per shown appointment.

Preliminary quotes aren't bind-ready. An MVR/CLUE pull surfaced an accident that accident-forgiveness had masked, wiping out a client's savings and keeping them put. Recommendation quality still depends on the agent's verification call: automation preps the renewal, the agent closes it.
